food_subgroup: Cooked prawn / shrimp dish with chilli sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Udang Belado; FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is the closest parent class covering cooked shrimp preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific belado or sambal prawn code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789004 covers prawn as a food substance; no specific belado preparation code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Udang Belado is approximately 200 g, comprising roughly 6–8 medium-to-large prawns coated in belado chilli sauce, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Prawns / Shrimp with Chilli Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Shrimp, cooked, NDB 15152) used in combination with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for belado sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo does not list a dedicated Udang Belado entry. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Sambal Prawns' (closest Singaporean dish equivalent) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for belado sauce (chilli, shallots, oil, tomato). The composite reflects a typical restaurant-style Udang Belado: prawns ~60% by weight, belado sauce ~40%.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp/prawn dish with chilli sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Udang Belado. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cooked seafood dishes with chilli sauce. The dish is predominantly protein and fat (prawns) with a small carbohydrate contribution from chilli sauce ingredients (tomato, sugar, shallots). GL per 200 g serving = (25 × 4.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.4 g, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce, as the belado sauce already contributes significant sodium (~840 mg per standard 200 g serving, representing approximately 37% of the 2300 mg daily limit).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate content (~4.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Udang Belado has a low glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of only 2.4 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large portions of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (~130 mg per 100 g) and sodium content from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol (~260 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion frequency and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to offset the sodium and cholesterol load from the belado preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Udang Belado is a Southeast Asian prawn dish originating from Minangkabau (West Sumatra, Indonesia) cuisine and widely adopted in Singapore's Malay and Indonesian food culture. The dish features whole or halved prawns (udang) cooked in a vibrant, fiery belado chilli paste made from ground red chillies, shallots, garlic, tomato, lime juice, salt, and a small amount of sugar, all sautéed in cooking oil. The result is a glossy, intensely red sauce that clings to the prawns, delivering a bold combination of heat, slight sweetness, and tangy acidity. The texture contrasts the firm, succulent prawn flesh with the thick, aromatic sauce. Nutritionally, Udang Belado is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, providing approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, 13.5 g protein, and 5.2 g fat. Its low glycemic index (~25) makes it suitable for blood sugar management. The sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Malay and Indonesian restaurants, hawker centres, and food courts, typically accompanied by steamed white rice or lontong (compressed rice cake). The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it accessible across multiple dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Udang Belado?

confidence_reason: No dedicated Udang Belado entry was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo Sambal Prawns (closest Singaporean equivalent) and USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA sauce component data. The adaptation is methodologically sound but introduces estimation uncertainty, justifying a medium confidence rating.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases consulted (HPB Foodo, USDA SR Legacy) for this dish type. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g, confirmed as negligible/trace in the source data for this preparation. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on cooked seafood with chilli sauce; no direct GI measurement for Udang Belado was located. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant-style preparation including belado sauce; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish originates from Minangkabau (Padang) cuisine of West Sumatra and is a staple in Singapore's Malay and Indonesian food scene. Price data from field survey data (SGD 10.00) is preserved from the input record for reference only and does not influence nutritional values.
